does: This paper investigates whether quantized language models that maintain acceptable perplexity actually preserve the interpretable features identified by sparse autoencoders. The authors freeze a SAE trained on full-precision models and measure how individual SAE features change when models are quantized to various bit-widths (INT8 through INT4) using round-to-nearest quantization. They compute per-feature survival rates by calculating Pearson correlation between full-precision and quantized activations on identical tokens, testing on Pythia-70M and Gemma-2-2B. The central result is that perplexity improvements can mask substantial feature degradation: for example, Gemma-2-2B at INT7 improves perplexity while destroying 18.7% of active SAE features, and at INT6 only 51.3% of features survive despite perplexity gains under sliding-window evaluation.
connects: This work builds on the interpretability research program using sparse autoencoders to understand language model internals, which has grown from work on mechanistic interpretability and feature-based analysis of neural networks. It responds to the practical deployment reality that quantization is ubiquitous for LLMs and that standard metrics like perplexity are used to validate quantized models. The paper extends recent work examining whether behavioral metrics capture true model fidelity by applying SAE-based measurement to the quantization setting. It connects to broader concerns about measuring model degradation beyond task performance and aligns with the interpretability community's push to develop richer evaluation criteria than downstream accuracy or perplexity alone.
enables: This work opens directions for developing quantization-aware training or fine-tuning methods that explicitly preserve interpretable features, not just task performance. It suggests that SAE feature survival could become a complementary evaluation metric for quantization schemes, potentially guiding the design of better quantization algorithms. The finding that feature degradation is graded rather than cliff-like motivates investigation into which features are most robust and why, which could inform more targeted quantization strategies. More broadly, it points toward using interpretability tools as a lens for quality assurance in model compression and deployment, and raises questions about whether other compression techniques like pruning or distillation similarly hide feature damage from standard metrics.
